title: Hello, JavaScript!
description: Write and run your first JavaScript program with console.log.
objectives: Print text to the output with console.log
Understand strings use single or double quotes
Run your code and see the result
recap: You wrote your first JavaScript program. `console.log(...)` prints its argument to the output, and strings can use either `"double"` or `'single'` quotes — pick whichever keeps the quotes inside your text easy to read.
practicePrompts: Print your name on one line and your favorite language on the next.
Print a string that contains a quotation mark inside it.

practiceExercises.title: Two lines
practiceExercises.prompt: Print "Hello" on one line and "World" on the next.
practiceExercises.goal: Use console.log twice to produce two lines of output.
practiceExercises.starterCode: // Print Hello and World on separate lines
hints: Each `console.log` call ends with its own newline.

practiceExercises.title: Quotes inside a string
practiceExercises.prompt: Print the sentence: She said "JavaScript is fun!" — with the double quotes visible in the output.
practiceExercises.goal: Choose quote styles so the quotes inside a string don't clash.
practiceExercises.starterCode: // Print the sentence with the quotes visible
hints: If your string has double quotes in it, what quote style should the outside of the string use?
Wrap the whole sentence in single quotes so the double quotes inside are just regular characters.

practiceExercises.title: Tiny ASCII art
practiceExercises.prompt: Print a small triangle of asterisks, three lines: *, **, ***.
practiceExercises.goal: Get comfortable running multiple console.log calls in a row.
hints: Three lines means three print calls.
Print `"*"`, then `"**"`, then `"***"` — each on its own line.
